title: Demonstrate by the Path of Righteousness and Peace!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy, not a replacement for the canonical source. Visual inspection restored the passage about liquor permits, illicit arrack, Gandhi's toddy-shop picketing, and Periyar's five hundred coconut trees, which the OCR had largely lost. It also confirmed that the source prints 20 February 1938, where the OCR read 1988. Only this translation copy has been corrected; the canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged.
paragraphs: In yesterday's “Letter to My Brethren,” dated 6 August 2015, I explained why the DMK Government was compelled to suspend prohibition in Tamil Nadu in 1971 and how prohibition was restored in 1974 by that same DMK Government. Yet some persist in repeating only that prohibition was lifted under DMK rule, because they must somehow find something with which to criticise the Kazhagam. They conceal the truth that prohibition was postponed because of Tamil Nadu's financial condition and was later brought back.

Was liquor not sold in Tamil Nadu before the DMK postponed prohibition in 1971? It certainly was. The very wealthy, the well-to-do, and even the middle classes obtained “permits” to drink and purchased different kinds of liquor from shops. Those with fewer means bought and drank illicit arrack. There was therefore no earlier period when liquor was completely absent. Even before Independence, the sale of liquor led Mahatma Gandhi to conduct a great agitation known as the toddy-shop picketing movement. Thanthai Periyar not only participated in toddy-shop picketing but cut down all five hundred coconut trees in his own grove from which toddy could have been tapped.

Although the DMK, after postponing prohibition in 1971, began the process of restoring it in 1973 and implemented it again in 1974, those hostile to the DMK repeatedly speak only of the 1971 postponement. Have people never made a decision according to the circumstances of a particular time, and then withdrawn or changed it when circumstances altered? While Perarignar Anna was alive, the DMK demanded a separate Dravida Nadu. Did it not later abandon that demand? Thanthai Periyar first demanded Dravida Nadu; after 1956, did he not alter it to “Tamil Nadu for Tamils”?

The Congress Government at the Centre declared Hindi the official language. Did it not later declare English an associate official language? Rajaji, who introduced prohibition in only four districts of the Madras Presidency in 1937, also made Hindi a compulsory subject. Did he not later oppose Hindi and thunder from public platforms alongside Anna? At that time, no one accused him merely by repeating that he had introduced Hindi as a subject in Tamil Nadu.

Why, has the PMK founder who now attacks us so fiercely not supported the DMK in one election and the AIADMK in the next? After contesting every election with the support of Dravidian parties, has he not now changed his position and declared that he will have no further relationship with either the DMK or AIADMK? And has this same Ramadoss, who speaks of prohibition in Tamil Nadu, ever once demanded prohibition in the neighbouring Union Territory of Puducherry?

Changing policies and positions over time according to the needs of the period, the future of a party and the welfare of the people is no mortal sin. One could cite a hundred examples from world history.

Let me give a few examples of decisions changing even on prohibition. In the United States, the Constitution itself was amended and prohibition introduced on 16 January 1919. But on 20 February 1938, prohibition was repealed there.

Finland enacted prohibition in 1907 and repealed it in 1932. Norway introduced prohibition in 1919 and repealed it in 1927. Newfoundland implemented prohibition in 1915 and repealed it in 1924. Turkey implemented it in 1920 and repealed it in 1924.

Thus, in many countries throughout the world, prohibition has been implemented only with repeated wavering. Why? Because it is prohibition, is it not? In my letter of the 6th, I quoted what I said in the 1971 Budget when prohibition was postponed. In that Budget I stated:

> By enforcing prohibition strictly in Tamil Nadu alone, we are losing the very opportunity to protect the discipline, integrity and honesty that we seek to preserve through prohibition. Therefore, although this Government continues to believe in the lofty and unique policy of prohibition, I regretfully announce that it has decided to suspend operation of the prohibition law in Tamil Nadu from 30 August, until the Union Government assumes responsibility for implementing this policy throughout India.
It should also not be forgotten that a Prohibition Propaganda Committee was formed under the leadership of our friend MGR.
Indeed, on 13 September 1969, our dear friend MGR issued a statement about prohibition. He concluded:

> I can declare more firmly than anyone else that the DMK leader and Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Kalaignar possesses greater concern and commitment to the conviction that prohibition is necessary for the people than any individual or party, not only in Tamil Nadu but anywhere in the Indian subcontinent.

He later wrote about this at length in his serial, “Why Was I Born?”, in *Ananda Vikatan*. Those now in the AIADMK may have forgotten it; I have not. Yet it was while this same dear friend MGR, who had been so firm on prohibition, was Chief Minister that the laws and schemes relating to prohibition were altered. Arrack and toddy shops were opened on 1 May 1981, though drinkers were required to obtain a “permit.”

Until 1981, Tamil Nadu had no factories manufacturing Indian-made foreign liquor. Only after 1981 were licences to manufacture such liquor granted to four private individuals and a cooperative-sector enterprise. Toddy shops were licensed by auction. Arrack production was given to ten private individuals, and wholesale distribution of arrack to fifteen private individuals. These are all events of the past.

Why have people now risen spontaneously in protest throughout Tamil Nadu? How did it begin? Gandhian Sasi Perumal was not even sixty years old. His protest sought only the closure of one TASMAC shop in Kanyakumari district. Had the Government agreed to close that one shop, the crisis would never have arisen and protests would not have spread across Tamil Nadu. But what has this Government ever attended to, that it should attend to this? How many hours could he remain atop a mobile-phone tower? Had the responsible officials arrived within an hour, discussed the issue and resolved it, would Sasi Perumal have died?

What is the position now? Eight days after his death, his body still cannot be buried. Having lost the head of their household, his family is spending its days between protest and prison. Yesterday, his eldest son filed a case in the Madras High Court seeking a judicial inquiry into his father's death, and the Court ordered notice to the Government. How many days have passed since I issued a statement demanding the same judicial inquiry? Did these rulers listen? Had they listened then, would matters have deteriorated until Sasi Perumal's own son had to approach the Court?

The issue that began in Kanyakumari district is now spreading like fire throughout Tamil Nadu. It is said that Emperor Nero played the fiddle while Rome burned. The AIADMK Government is behaving in exactly that fashion. Do you know the result? Amid protests across the State, a TASMAC shop at Pudhupalayam near Vazhapadi in Salem district was set on fire. An employee named Selvam, trapped inside, lost his life.
Periyasamy, former MLA and State President of the Tamil Nadu TASMAC Employees' Association, said:

> The Government bears complete responsibility for the incident at Vazhapadi. Employees should have been sent home when their work ended. In a situation where a law-and-order problem had arisen, this incident could not have occurred if police protection had been provided during the night. The Government must accept full responsibility.

I convey my profound condolences to the family of Selvam, who died because of this Government's negligence. Who must accept responsibility for all this? Is there a minister for this department or not? Is issuing statements attacking every opposition party his only job? The Chief Minister acts against all manner of ministers. What is the mystery behind her patience with this minister despite his successive failures?
What happens under this Government? Newspapers report as news that the Chief Minister came to the Secretariat after seven days. *Dinamalar* reported on 4 August 2015:

> Chief Minister Jayalalithaa last came to the Secretariat on the 27th. Thereafter, yesterday (3 August 2015), she arrived at 12.35 p.m. and was received by the ministers. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa inaugurated rooms for nursing mothers at bus stands. She consulted ministers and officials about the law-and-order situation. Davidson Devasirvatham, who has assumed office as Inspector General of Intelligence; Arun, who has taken charge as Joint Commissioner of Police, Chennai South Zone; and Ravikumar, Additional Commissioner of Police, Chennai North Zone, met the Chief Minister and received her greetings. After the events ended, the Chief Minister departed the Secretariat at 1.25 p.m.

Thus the Chief Minister, arriving at the Secretariat after a week, remained there for only fifty minutes, and we are told that she accomplished all these tasks within that time! The Chief Minister comes once a week, launches various schemes, has photographs taken, and then the Government releases those photographs and press notes one by one on successive days, as though she came to the Secretariat and launched a scheme every day. The Chief Secretary and Adviser appear in the photographs as accomplices. Only when another government comes tomorrow and asks whether they too were parties to these irregularities will the truth be understood.

At the demonstrations to be held on the 10th under the leadership of the DMK District Secretaries, the details I set out yesterday and today must be explained to the public. As I stated in my first announcement, the demonstrations must follow the path of righteousness and proceed peacefully. We must create a situation in which the demonstrations conducted by the Kazhagam are recognised as a complete success.
With affection,
M. K. 7 August 2015
